title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a medical researcher called Dr. Julia Vance talking about the use of artificial intelligence in healthcare. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a medical researcher called Dr. Julia Vance talking about the use of artificial intelligence in healthcare. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Dr. Julia Vance discusses how AI is transforming diagnostics and the challenges of data privacy.
extracts.audio_script: Good morning. My name is Dr. Julia Vance. I’ve spent the last decade working at the intersection of medicine and technology. Artificial Intelligence is no longer a futuristic concept; it’s a tool we use every day to save lives. [7]I first realized the potential of AI after seeing a demonstration of an automated diagnostic tool[/7] that could identify skin cancer with 99% accuracy. It was a game-changer. One of the most significant benefits is speed. [8]AI can analyze thousands of medical images in a fraction of the time a human doctor would take[/8], allowing for much earlier interventions. However, the technology is not without its controversies. [9]I found that the most common concern among patients was the issue of data privacy[/9]. People are naturally worried about who has access to their sensitive health information. We have to be incredibly transparent about how we use and protect this data. Regarding the implementation, it's not just about software. [10]We are currently testing a series of robotic surgical arms that can perform complex procedures with unmatched precision[/10]. It’s fascinating to watch. My daily routine has changed significantly. [11]The unexpected challenge of my current role is the amount of time I spend in legal meetings[/11] discussing ethics and regulations. It’s a side of the job I didn't anticipate. Despite the complexity, the results are worth it. [12]Observing the successful recovery of our first patient treated using an AI-guided plan was truly moving[/12]. It validated years of hard work. Looking to the future, [13]I believe that personalized medicine is the ultimate goal of AI in healthcare[/13]. Every treatment will be tailored to the individual's genetic code. As we often say in the lab, [14]we are not replacing doctors, we are 'empowering healers' with the best possible data[/14]. It’s an exciting time to be in medicine.

dialogue.speaker: julia
dialogue.text: Good morning. My name is Dr. Julia Vance. I’ve spent the last decade working at the intersection of medicine and technology. Artificial Intelligence is no longer a futuristic concept; it’s a tool we use every day to save lives. I first realized the potential of AI after seeing a demonstration of an automated diagnostic tool that could identify skin cancer with 99% accuracy. It was a game-changer. One of the most significant benefits is speed. AI can analyze thousands of medical images in a fraction of the time a human doctor would take, allowing for much earlier interventions. However, the technology is not without its controversies. I found that the most common concern among patients was the issue of data privacy. People are naturally worried about who has access to their sensitive health information. We have to be incredibly transparent about how we use and protect this data. Regarding the implementation, it's not just about software. We are currently testing a series of robotic surgical arms that can perform complex procedures with unmatched precision. It’s fascinating to watch. My daily routine has changed significantly. The unexpected challenge of my current role is the amount of time I spend in legal meetings discussing ethics and regulations. It’s a side of the job I didn't anticipate. Despite the complexity, the results are worth it. Observing the successful recovery of our first patient treated using an AI-guided plan was truly moving. It validated years of hard work. Looking to the future, I believe that personalized medicine is the ultimate goal of AI in healthcare. Every treatment will be tailored to the individual's genetic code. As we often say in the lab, we are not replacing doctors, we are 'empowering healers' with the best possible data. It’s an exciting time to be in medicine.

questions.question: Julia’s interest in AI was triggered by a presentation of an (7) .
questions.explanation: She mentions a demonstration of an automated diagnostic tool for skin cancer.

questions.question: The main advantage of using AI in medicine is that it can process (8) much faster than humans.
questions.explanation: She says AI can analyze thousands of medical images in a fraction of the time.

questions.question: According to Julia, (9) is the topic that patients worry about the most.
questions.explanation: She found that the most common concern among patients was data privacy.

questions.question: The hospital is currently experimenting with (10) as a way to improve surgery.
questions.explanation: They are testing robotic surgical arms that perform procedures with precision.

questions.question: Julia admits that she was surprised by the number of (11) she has to attend.
questions.explanation: She mentions the unexpected challenge of the amount of time spent in legal meetings.

questions.question: Julia found the outcome of the first (12) to be a very emotional experience.
questions.explanation: The recovery of the first patient treated using an AI-guided plan was moving.

questions.question: Julia thinks that (13) will be the most important development in AI healthcare.
questions.explanation: She believes personalized medicine is the ultimate goal.

questions.question: Julia uses the phrase (14) to describe how technology assists medical professionals.
questions.explanation: She says they are 'empowering healers' with data, not replacing them.
